# Questions in Livestream 168

Live Stream #168: Funky, Groovy, Trendy Ramesses II

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=391" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:31</a> | What do you know about Luwian hieroglyphs? | He treats Luwian as a real hieroglyphic language and recommends Payne's teaching grammar as a starting point. | He says the script is properly connected with Luwian, used roughly from 1500 to 700 BC, especially for monumental inscriptions and seals. He describes it as a real hieroglyphic writing system with phonetic signs, logograms, and determinatives, distinct from Hittite even though it is sometimes called Hittite hieroglyphs. He is not an expert, but recommends Annick Payne's grammar because it includes glyph tables, grammar, and example texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=540" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:00</a> | Is there a standard way to spell Ramesses? | Yes. He says the usual Egyptological spelling keeps the core consonants and commonly appears as "Ramesses." | He says the spelling in the question is the standard English Egyptological form, while acknowledging that other spellings exist. The essential point is preserving the r, m, and at least two s consonants, because the second s is present in Egyptian and preserved in the biblical text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=615" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:15</a> | On some versions of Manetho's list there is a Kbrom or Keos. Who was that? | He treats it as a manuscript or copyist problem rather than a real king attested in Egyptian king lists. | He says the figure appears only in Manetho, placed between Ahmose and Amenhotep I, where Egyptian records leave no room for another king. Since the name does not appear in the Turin Canon, Ramesses' Abydos list, Seti I's Abydos list, or other Egyptian king lists, he thinks it is best explained as a corrupted manuscript, scribal error, or similar mistake. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=750" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:30</a> | Was the serpent's temptation of Eve the first time Satan sinned? | He says in all likelihood yes, connecting the answer to Ezekiel 28. | He reads Ezekiel 28 as locating Satan's fall in Eden, where iniquity is found in him. He allows that other angels may have fallen earlier, but says Satan's own first sin appears to be the garden episode, so he joins an already existing rebellious population at that point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=840" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:00</a> | Is there a relationship between Genesis 3, Ezekiel 28, and Isaiah 14:12-15 as Heiser argued? | He says the relationship is debated and handles the passages carefully rather than simply equating all three. | He agrees that the passages are connected, but not necessarily in the exact way Heiser reads them. He sees Genesis 3 and Ezekiel 28 as strongly linked because Ezekiel moves beyond the king of Tyre to an Edenic covering cherub whose unrighteousness is found in him. Isaiah 14 has a weaker connection, because it uses a similar pattern with the king of Babylon, but he treats it more cautiously. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=1071" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:51</a> | Is there a distinction between Eden and the Garden of Eden? | He says no; in the biblical text they are the same place. | He treats Eden and the Garden of Eden as synonymous in the text. This comes during his discussion of Ezekiel 28 and Genesis 3, where he argues that Ezekiel's Eden language connects directly with the garden episode rather than a separate location. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=1500" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:00</a> | Wasn't Satan a liar and murderer from the beginning? | He says "the beginning" in that argument is the beginning of biblical history, centered on Genesis. | He says the relevant beginning is Genesis and the garden narrative, not a claim that Satan was created fallen. He explicitly says Satan fell, that Ezekiel 28 is about that fall, and that God does not create anyone fallen; angelic beings become fallen through agency and volition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=1746" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:06</a> | If universalism is unbiblical, does it rise to the level of heresy? | He says universalism is unbiblical, but the heresy question needs careful definition. | He defines heresy as an error so grievous that it affects salvation, using denial of the Trinity or salvation by grace through faith as examples. He says universalism is wrong and unbiblical, but because there are different forms, including hopeful universalism and restorationist forms, it is not automatically heresy by itself. It can become heretical when joined to doctrines such as Unitarianism that deny who Christ is. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=1932" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:12</a> | Do you consider the 430 years of Exodus 12 compatible with the span from Hammurabi to year 25 of Ramesses II? | Broadly yes. He argues the biblical count can still fit because the clock is tied to the patriarchal promise tradition rather than that single political benchmark. | He says the issue is not simply the distance from Hammurabi to Ramesses II year 25, because the clock begins with the promise to Abraham and the relevant promise may not be precisely dated. He also factors in Isaac's time in Syria as not counting toward the Egypt-Canaan span, producing roughly 450 years and leaving a manageable gap. His conclusion is that the chronology is approximate, but the biblical narrative has enough internal time to cover the difference. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=2162" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:02</a> | Are we fairly certain Avaris was abandoned during the reign of Ramesses II rather than under Merneptah or later? | Yes. He places the abandonment in mid-Dynasty 19 and says that points most directly to Ramesses II. | He says the abandonment is followed by a cemetery whose surviving remains date to mid-Dynasty 19. Since Ramesses II ruled for 66 years and dominates the middle of that roughly 110-year dynasty, a mid-Dynasty 19 date leaves very little room for anyone else. That is why he says the evidence points specifically to Ramesses II rather than Merneptah or a later ruler. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=2423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:23</a> | What does Acts 8:39a mean when the Spirit snatched Philip away? | He reads it as a reference to divine transport after Philip baptized the Ethiopian eunuch. | He reads the passage as Philip being carried away by the Holy Spirit after the baptism, not merely walking off unnoticed. He compares it with rare biblical precedents such as Enoch being taken by God and Elijah being taken in 2 Kings 2, and says commentators generally treat it as an example of the Spirit moving someone where needed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=2587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:07</a> | What themes of *The Lord of the Rings* did Peter Jackson miss? | He says Jackson misses how the Ring embodies Sauron as deceiver and how it exposes different temptations in different characters. | He says Jackson treated the Ring too much as a generic source of evil, whereas Tolkien presents it as bound up with Sauron as deceiver and tempter of nations. In his reading, the Ring works like temptation and sin, drawing out different desires in different people: possession in Gollum, vanity in Galadriel, and political power in Boromir. He connects that to Tolkien's Catholic worldview. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=2929" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:49</a> | What is a casserole, and why tuna? | He defines tuna casserole as a baked pasta dish and strongly approves of it. | He describes tuna casserole as macaroni baked with tuna, cream of mushroom soup, vegetables such as carrots, celery, onions, and peas, plus cheddar cheese and breadcrumbs. The exchange is playful, but he gives a direct food answer and says he loves tuna casserole even though some people dislike it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=3282" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:42</a> | Why is Cyril of Alexandria considered a saint? | He says Cyril wrote extensively and well on biblical and theological topics, while noting the broader historical complexity. | He says Cyril is honored because he wrote voluminously and with great theological skill, especially on biblical topics and the Trinity. He also explicitly distinguishes that from Cyril's personal or historical flaws, saying Cyril could be an excellent theologian while still being unpleasant in other ways. Cyril's influence in Coptic and Eastern Orthodox contexts is part of why he is regarded as a saint. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=3367" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:07</a> | How were Ai, Hazor, and Jericho military bases? | He distinguishes them: Ai was a watchpost, Hazor was a military center, and Jericho is less certain. | He says Ai was in all likelihood a military watchpost between Jericho and Bethel rather than a normal city. Hazor was the capital of a northern Amorite confederacy and therefore a major military center, even if not simply a base. Jericho was larger and had civilians, but military bases could have support populations, so he treats a military function there as plausible rather than certain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=3559" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:19</a> | Has he changed his opinion on a debated Hebrew reading involving Kesh, an ending, and possible resh-daleth confusion? | No. The transcript is unclear, but he says either textual explanation could be right and he is not certain. | The question wording is garbled in the transcript, so the row preserves only what the answer supports. He says one possibility is Kesh with an ending added, while another is a form where resh was changed to daleth because the letters can look similar in Hebrew. He does not choose between them and says the only point he is confident about is that the form begins with Kesh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=3674" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:14</a> | Does joking that Satan is a squatter conflict with Jude 8 and similar passages? | He says people often give Satan more credit than he deserves, and the relevant passages need to be read in context. | He explains Jude 5-8 as a structured set of parallel examples, then says Jude 8's language about reviling glories is not simply a ban on every dismissive comment about Satan. In his view, joking that Satan is a squatter is an observation rather than a railing judgment like the one Michael refuses to pronounce in Jude 9. The point is to avoid giving Satan more status than the text gives him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=4016" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:56</a> | What is the difference between exegetical and pastoral commentaries if pastors are supposed to exegete the text? | He says exegetical commentaries focus more directly on text-critical and interpretive details, while pastoral ones aim at sermon and ministry use. | He says the difference is focus. Exegetical commentaries work in the original languages and try to draw out the meaning of the text, while pastoral commentaries concentrate on application. For preaching, he frames the task as reading, explaining, and applying the text: exegetical commentaries help most with understanding and explaining, while pastoral commentaries can help when the preacher needs guidance applying a difficult passage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=4296" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:36</a> | Does the channel need an Instagram account for shorts? | He makes no promise and says he does not feel much urge to add Instagram. | He jokes that Twitter, TikTok, YouTube, and Discord already create a long weekly channel workload. Since he is not an Instagram user and does not currently feel drawn to it, he says he is not promising to start an Instagram account at that point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=4457" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:17</a> | Is he going to appear on IP's Exodus stream? | No. He says he was not planning on it and had not watched the referenced video. | He says he has heard enough about the video to expect the usual problems from that group, including reliance on minimalist scholars and factual mistakes, but he has not watched it himself. His answer is that he was not planning to get involved in the stream. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=4556" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:56</a> | How do you overcome the sorrow of moving on to a next place in ministry when you know God wants you to leave? | He says to trust God's call, make the move, and avoid lingering in the old ministry. | He frames that sorrow as a good kind of grief when the previous ministry was fruitful, but says obedience matters if God is calling someone onward. His practical counsel is to make the departure clear, transition swiftly and decisively, not hang around in a way that harms the successor, and trust God to heal the sorrow. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=5504" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:44</a> | What is the best way to get started doing historical research without a history degree? | He recommends focusing on a specific period or question, learning the sources, and building familiarity with the scholarship. | He recommends starting with a narrow period or topic that genuinely interests the researcher, then reading heavily enough to build the kind of background a degree would normally provide. The practical standard he gives is being able to tell good sources from cranks, recognize important works in footnotes, and evaluate source quality before moving into archives or original research. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=6025" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:25</a> | In Ezekiel 20:25, what were the laws that were not good? | He says the passage contrasts God's ordinances with the consequences of Israel rejecting them, rather than God commanding evil as good. | He reads the whole chapter as contrasting God's ordinances with the ordinances of Israel's fathers. God repeatedly commands Israel to give up idols, abominations, and profaned Sabbaths, while Israel chooses its own idolatrous practices. On his reading, the "not good" statutes are viewed from the disobedient people's perspective: they reject God's commands as not good because they prefer idolatry and even child sacrifice, which God then pronounces unclean and desolating. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=6878" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:38</a> | Why would Moses have sent his wife and sons back in Exodus 18 instead of bringing them along? | He treats the passage as reflecting practical and narrative concerns around Moses' mission and family movement. | He says Moses was returning to Egypt to confront Pharaoh and create political trouble, so bringing his wife and children would have made them targets. He imagines not only Pharaoh but also officials or soldiers taking matters into their own hands against Moses' family. Sending Zipporah and the children away kept them out of harm's way, and he notes that Zipporah was capable and the children were in good hands. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=7001" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:41</a> | What is the state of the debate on the historicity of David and the United Monarchy? | He says most scholars now accept a historical David, with debate focused more on the size and extent of his kingdom. | He says the extreme minimalist claim that David or Israel never existed no longer represents the consensus. In his summary, the current debate is about the extent of David's kingdom: whether it was local, how far it reached beyond Judah, and how "united" the monarchy was. Details such as the Goliath story or the geographic reach of David's rule remain debated, but the existence of a historical David is not the main fight anymore. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=7184" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:44</a> | What was the purpose of nail clays in Mesopotamia? | He explains clay nails as inscribed foundation or dedicatory objects connected with building and ritual contexts. | He describes clay nails as fired, cone-shaped objects, often with cuneiform inscriptions, that were driven into mud brick walls. They could contain prayers, blessings, spells, curses, or notes connected with a temple or building context, and were intended to be hidden within the wall. He adds that Egypt had comparable objects and that the examples he has seen were larger than one might expect. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=7481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:41</a> | What do we know about the wise men who visited Jesus, and were they Zoroastrian priests? | He says his view has changed: he no longer thinks they were Zoroastrian priests and now leans toward Asia Minor, while staying non-dogmatic. | He says the Zoroastrian explanation is weakened because Zoroastrians did not practice astrology. He now thinks the "east" language could point toward Anatolia or Turkey and perhaps remnants of Neo-Hittite religion, since that cultural setting had astrology and astral deities. He stresses that this is a current leaning, not a settled dogmatic conclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=8553" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:33</a> | How did first-century Roman converts keep the seventh-day Sabbath with their eight-day week? | He says they did it with difficulty by tracking the seven-day cycle across the local calendar. | He explains that the mismatch between Roman and Jewish weekly cycles could create practical problems, especially for poorer laborers who had to work when wealthier believers could gather early. He says Jewish and early Christian communities could track the seven-day Sabbath even under Roman or Egyptian calendar systems, but after Christians were pushed out of synagogue and temple settings, Sunday worship continued while Sabbath worship as such ceased to be required. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=8946" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:06</a> | If Origen was condemned partly for universalism, does that show universalism, or his brand of it, is heresy? | He says the council history is more complicated and does not by itself settle the question. | He notes that councils sometimes condemned things for reasons he would not treat as salvation-level heresy, using icon veneration as an example. He says Origen was controversial for more than universalism, including associations with subordinationism and later Arian developments, and also criticizes Origen's allegorical method while acknowledging his brilliance. On this answer, Origen's condemnation is relevant but not a simple proof that universalism alone is heresy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=9397" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:37</a> | Did Egyptians have specific beliefs about dreams and nightmares? | He says yes, Egypt had dream interpretation traditions, while details vary and require careful source work. | He points to Chester Beatty III, the Egyptian Dream Book, as a catalog of dreams and interpretations marked good or bad. He explains that Egyptian dream interpretation was not supposed to be improvised: a priest would consult the dream book and match the dream to its listed interpretation. He uses Joseph's interpretation of the cupbearer's dream as an example where Joseph gives an interpretation opposite to the Egyptian catalog and is vindicated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=9943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:43</a> | Did Babylon also have a dream book or anything connected to Daniel's dream interpreting? | He says he is not sure and would need to look it up. | The transcript only supports a limited answer here: he says he is not sure whether Babylon had a dream book or a parallel source relevant to Daniel, and that he would have to look it up. He does not offer a substantive conclusion in this livestream segment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=10005" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:45</a> | How can someone get a purchased book signed by the author? | He says the person would need to bring or send it through an appropriate opportunity rather than assuming an automatic signing path. | He suggests practical in-person options, especially meeting him at ETS in San Diego in November or bringing the book on the Egypt tour. He says mailing a book to him for signing and return would not be financially practical, so a chance meeting or conference setting is the better path. For tour participants, he jokes that he has promised a custom drawing and hieroglyphic inscription as well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=10849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:49</a> | Was there ever a pharaoh who failed the trials of the Sed festival and was removed or killed? | No known pharaoh failed it; if one had, he says the remedy would have been retirement rather than being killed or ousted. | He explains the Sed festival as a ritual in the king's thirtieth regnal year, involving an obstacle course or running display meant to show that the king was still healthy enough to rule. He says there was no time limit, so failure was not framed as losing a timed contest; the king simply had to complete it. He knows of no record of a pharaoh failing it, but says a theoretical failure would have led to a formal declaration that the king could no longer rule and a regent taking over, not execution or forcible removal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=11352" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:12</a> | What are his thoughts on Ezekiel 20:25-26 as referring to human sacrifice and the claim that Molech was a ritual rather than a deity? | He rejects simplistic readings and says the passage concerns Israel rejecting God's ordinances and receiving bad judgments. | He says Ezekiel 20:25-26 does involve human sacrifice in verse 26, but not as something God endorses or commands. He has no objection to understanding Molech as a ritual rather than a deity and says Ezekiel 20 itself does not mention Molech. His criticism is directed at readings that claim Yahweh commanded child sacrifice; in context, he says the passage condemns abominations, idol worship, and profaned Sabbaths, then says God pronounced the people unclean because of those gifts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=11849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:29</a> | Is the difference between a redaction and a scribal gloss that a gloss adds context while a redaction changes the content? | Broadly yes. He says a scribal gloss adds explanation, while redaction changes the wording or meaning. | He uses Ezekiel 20 as an example: a gloss might insert an explanatory phrase such as identifying the fire as an altar to Molech, while leaving the underlying sentence intact. A redaction, by contrast, would alter the content itself, such as changing a word so the sentence means something different. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=12869" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:29</a> | Is it true archaeology proves Yahweh as a covenant name predated the Exodus? | He says no solid proof exists, though there is ambiguous suggestive evidence. | He points to the Soleb temple inscription and the Shasu of Yahu as a possible pre-Exodus reference from the time of Amenhotep III, but says the evidence is not strong because it lacks a theophoric determinative. He thinks the name probably did predate the Exodus, but says the archaeological case remains ambiguous rather than proven. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=12972" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:12</a> | What is his favorite ice cream? | He says praline, specifically praline ice cream from the University of Wisconsin. | He names praline ice cream from the University of Wisconsin-Madison dairy, describing it as the best ice cream he has had. He recounts seeing a long line in a Wisconsin winter, being told by a professor that the ice cream was worth trying, and later finding the praline flavor exceptionally rich and better than other commercial ice creams he mentions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=13228" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:28</a> | How many biblical euphemisms are in the Bible, and what are some funny or unusual ones? | He says he does not know the total, then gives examples of euphemistic biblical language. | He says he does not know how many biblical euphemisms there are. The example that comes to mind is the expression "cut a treaty," which he explains by picturing a scroll being unrolled, a portion cut off, and the treaty written on it. He treats that as an illustrative and interesting idiom rather than giving a complete catalog. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=13283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:23</a> | Between Super Mario, Sonic the Hedgehog, Crash Bandicoot, and Rayman, who is David's favorite and why? | He says none of them, because he has not played those games. | He explains that he never owned a video console, so he did not play those console-centered games. His own gaming history was mostly on PC, especially open-world and role-playing games such as the Ultima series. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=13757" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:17</a> | How does he take his eggs? | He used to prefer them poached with hollandaise on smoked salmon and a croissant, but now eats them more modestly. | He says that poached eggs with hollandaise sauce, smoked salmon, and a croissant was the way he used to love them. He also says he likes eggs in many forms, including poached eggs, omelets, steamed eggs, and even raw quail egg yolk in a sushi context, but notes that his current habits are more modest. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=13910" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:50</a> | Did ancient Egyptians eat eggs, and what was their word for egg? | Yes. He says they ate eggs, especially duck and goose eggs, then gives the Egyptian word after looking it up. | He says Egyptians did eat eggs, especially duck and goose eggs, and comments that duck eggs are larger and richer than chicken eggs. After looking it up, he gives the Egyptian word as "suhat" and spells it in the chat as s-w-hat, with the h described as a round h. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=14028" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:48</a> | Do the channel's custom emojis need more characters? | He jokes through the suggestion and rejects adding glittering vampires. | He says no to glittering vampires, then lists existing custom emojis such as an alien, a sound fox, Mr. EC, Merneptah, the Velocirabbit, and a scarab. He explains that YouTube limits the number of custom emojis and that the number of available slots depends on membership tier, so the channel cannot add unlimited characters. He jokes about possible lore-based options, including a "Chad GPT" emoji from a recent Discord incident. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/g9tKk48D9Rw?t=14851" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:31</a> | Did the Apostle John write Revelation, or did another John write it? | He says the author is certainly named John, but the precise identification with the Apostle John remains uncertain. | He says early church history mentions John the Apostle and John the Presbyter, and it is uncertain whether they are the same person or two different people. Revelation was written by someone named John who was known to the early church, and the Apostle John lived long enough that he could have written it. Still, he distinguishes that possibility from certainty, saying the Gospel of John is known as apostolic but Revelation's exact John is less certain. |
